svg.title: EM2 Wake Flow For The Keypad
svg.desc: A static flow diagram showing how the keypad wakes the device from EM2 and how KEYSCAN identifies the exact key after wake.
svg.text: EM2 Wake Flow For The Keypad
The GPIO interrupt is the wake mechanism, not the decode mechanism. The exact key is identified only after KEYSCAN is restored and scans the matrix again.
1. app_keypad_enter_em2_wake_mode() prepares the keypad for sleep

text.tspan: The code stops KEYSCAN, disables KEYSCAN column routes, drives the column pins low as ordinary GPIO,
and configures PA4 through PA7 as input-pull pins with falling-edge interrupts.

svg.text: 2. A key press wakes the device electrically

text.tspan: When the user presses a key, one row is pulled low through the pressed switch because the columns are being held low.
That falling edge triggers the row GPIO interrupt.

svg.text: 3. The ISR records wake state and posts EVENT_APP_KEYPAD_WAKE

text.tspan: The ISR stores a pending row mask and asks the ZAF app event distributor to process a wake event later.
This keeps the interrupt handler small and fast.
At ISR time, the code only knows that a row changed.
It does not know the final exact key yet.

svg.text: 4. EM2 exit restores the true scan path

text.tspan: The power transition callback leaves EM2, app_keypad_leave_em2_wake_mode() restores KEYSCAN GPIO modes
and routes, and sl_keyscan_driver_start_scan() resumes matrix scanning.

svg.text: 5. KEYSCAN now identifies the exact key and the normal path resumes

text.tspan: app.c dispatches EVENT_APP_KEYPAD_WAKE, app_keypad_process_wake_event() starts the wake guard, and KEYSCAN
performs a real matrix scan that the decode path turns into the final logical key.
